data.title: Gavin Newsom is ruining California — don't let him do it to the whole country
data.description:
How can anyone (other than maybe his immediate family) consider Gavin Newsom (D) a promising presidential candidate?  

Since Newsom became governor of California in 2019, he has presided over the ongoing decline of one of our country’s greatest states. California is rapidly losing population, losing businesses, and suffering repeated budget crises.

At a time when voters are concerned about affordability, California ranks as the second most expensive state in the U.S., thanks to high taxes, suffocating regulations and damaging progressive policies like the renewable energy mandates embraced by Newsom. It isn’t an accident that gasoline costs almost 50 percent more in California than the national average. 

Most recently, the threat of the nation’s first wealth tax being imposed on billionaires has the state’s most successful innovators departing in droves, taking their entrepreneurial genius and future endeavors with them.

Newsom has also shown himself to be a disastrous manager. California’s efforts to build a high-speed railroad have become a national joke. The estimated price tag for the hugely scaled-down project has skyrocketed from its original $33 billion 17 years ago to $135 billion now.

More recently, in the wake of Los Angeles’ disastrous wildfires (themselves reportedly at least partially the fault of Newsom’s poor forest oversight), rebuilding has been scant, despite bold promises from the governor. Some 16,000 homes were destroyed; so far there have only been 4,700 applications filed by those wanting to rebuild, and only 2,000 approved. That failure prompted President Trump to issue an executive order recently clearing the way for a rare federal takeover, promising to speed up permitting and construction.    

The final page in Newsom’s bleak record may turn out to be the discovery of massive fraud in his state. As in Minnesota, welfare programs in California appear to have been pilfered by thieves, all while Democrats in charge of the state looked the other way. A local federal prosecutor, calling Newsom the “king of fraud”, described one possible problem recently on a Fox News program. “California has spent $24 billion in the last five years on homelessness,” he pointed out. “And no one can account for where that money has really gone.”

Taxpayers don’t much like that; the exposure of fraud is what caused Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) to step back from running for a third term. Similar revelations could derail Newsom’s ambitions.

What exactly, other than an admittedly excellent head of hair, does Newsom bring to the table?

Certainly a boatload of Electoral College votes, but nearly any sentient Democrat can count on the deep-blue Golden State falling into his or her column. Maybe it’s the governor’s ability to shift with political winds. When the country seemed to oppose transgender athletes competing in women’s sports, for example, Newsom at least began to turn in that direction as well.  

Newsom’s competition for the 2028 Democratic nomination is not world-class. National polling shows Kamala Harris, a truly terrible campaigner, trailing just behind Newsom today. Others in the field include Pete Buttigieg, last seen whiffing on his responsibilities as Transportation secretary while on extended paternity leave during the COVID emergency, and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.), the Tik Tok contender who scares moderate Democrats to death. For what it’s worth, Polymarket has Newsom ahead by a mile, with AOC a distant second.

Since Newsom is leading, scrutiny of his accomplishments is warranted, including a review of his report card from the people who live in his state.

Last year, California led the U.S. in losing domestic migrants, with 229,077 leaving for greener pastures on net. From 2020 to 2024, California added more than 900,000 international migrants, but lost 1.46 million net domestic migrants, for a net loss of nearly a half million residents. Over the last 24 years, more than 4 million net domestic migrants have moved away.

People who study such things are surprised. In 2007, demographers estimated that, by 2050, California’s population would swell to 60 million. Today, the 2050 projection is for just 40 million. That’s what happens when you don’t treat your citizens well.  

The upshot of California’s population loss is that the state is poised to become less powerful politically. In the 2020 census, California lost one House seat; one group forecasts that, should current trends continue, the state will lose 4 more, dropping to 48 from the current 52.   

And if the Census were to stop including people in the country illegally — a change the Trump administration is trying to make — then California, home to more than 2 million undocumented people, would be much worse off. Democratic politicians have enticed illegal immigrants into their states by offering welfare benefits, access to drivers’ licenses and other perks, in part to maintain population numbers and thus political clout, even as residents flee for better-run states.

The vast number of illegal aliens living in California has put pressure on the state’s budget, since Newsom and his Democrat colleagues proudly provide free health care and education to all, regardless of citizenship status. As reported in the Los Angeles Times last March: “Gov. Gavin Newsom touted California’s role as the first state in the nation to offer healthcare to all income-eligible immigrants one year ago.” Unfortunately for Golden State residents, the cost of that healthcare, cited at $9.5 billion, was “already more than $3 billion above the budget estimate from last summer and is expected to grow.”

That’s not Newsom’s only budget problem. Notwithstanding being at the epicenter of a monster tech AI boom, California’s nonpartisan Legislative Analyst’s Office recently forecast the state will confront an $18 billion budget deficit this year, the fourth year in a row of yawning gaps. The numbers, according to that group, are only going to get worse.  

Newsom has made California smaller, poorer and less politically powerful. His many failings should be taken seriously; his presidential ambitions should not. 

Liz Peek is a former partner of major bracket Wall Street firm Wertheim and Company.

data.title: When will I get my tax refund from the IRS?
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – After months of Trump administration officials forecasting “very large refunds” in the 2026 filing season, early taxpayers may be wondering when that money will hit their accounts.

The average refund last year was $3,167. This year, analysts have projected it could be $1,000 higher, thanks to changes in tax law. More than 165 million individual income tax returns were processed last year, with 94% submitted electronically.

People with straightforward returns should not encounter delays, but because of an exodus of IRS workers since the start of the Trump administration, the national taxpayer advocate has cautioned that the 2026 tax filing season is likely to present challenges for those who run into problems filing.

“Among the reasons the 2025 filing season went well was that the IRS had its largest workforce in many years and faced no major tax law changes that required implementation during the filing season,” National Taxpayer Advocate Erin M. Collins explained. “Entering 2026, the landscape is markedly different. The IRS is simultaneously confronting a reduction of 27% of its workforce, leadership turnover, and the implementation of extensive and complex tax law changes mandated by the [One Big Beautiful Bill] Act, many of which apply retroactively and require significant IRS programming, guidance, changes to tax forms and instructions, and taxpayer education.”

In 2025, the IRS processed over 165 million individual tax returns, according to Collins, with customer service representatives – a team that has been “substantially” reduced – handling more than 100 million telephone calls.

When to expect a refund

If you file electronically, the IRS says it should take 21 days or less to receive your refund. If you choose direct deposit, it should take even less time.

“If at all possible, file electronically, and request that you receive your refund by direct deposit,” tax attorney Adam Brewer told Nexstar. “I know no one really loves to give their banking information to the IRS or to the state, but if you want your refund quickly, you have to give them the direct deposit information.”

If you file a paper return, the refund could take four weeks or more, and if your return requires amendments or corrections, it could take longer.

The average refund last year was $3,167. This year, analysts have projected it could be $1,000 higher, thanks to changes in tax law.

How to check the status of your refund

Taxpayers can use the online tool Where’s My Refund? to check the status of their refund within 24 hours of e-filing and generally within four weeks of filing a paper return. The “Where’s My Refund?” tool will also provide projected deposit dates for most early EITC/ACTC refund filers by Feb. 21, according to the IRS.

Information related to this tool is updated once daily, overnight. To access the status of your refund, you’ll need:

— Your Social Security or individual taxpayer ID number (ITIN)

Taxpayers can also consult the IRS2Go app, or their IRS Individual Online Account, to check their refund status.

The 2026 tax season runs through April 15.

The Associated Press contributed to this report.

data.title: Trump should push now for a convention of states to consider 3 new amendments
data.description:
More than 10 years after its delivery, warnings from Donald Trump’s June 2015 presidential candidacy announcement continue to resonate. He decried the high cost of the Affordable Care Act medical insurance subsidies. He sounded a warning about the federal debt, then at $18 trillion, calling $24 trillion in debt the point of no return.

About 15 months later, beginning with speeches in October 2016, he added a third dire warning about Washington’s entrenched bureaucracy. His call for a Constitutional amendment for term limits on Congress, and his phrase “drain the swamp,” drew huge positive reaction to crowds attending campaign rallies.

As this month ends, a cataclysmic controversy over the Dept. of Homeland Security threatens another partial government shutdown. The federal debt sits at more than $38.5 trillion. Despite the efforts of DOGE and actions which have dismantled some parts of government, such as the Department of Education — entrenched bureaucrats still hold massive power in and around the Beltway.

Another paralyzing problem is the vexing overreach of the judiciary. According to the non-profit Lawfare, there are now 253 active court cases challenging Trump’s administration actions as president. And although Supreme Court rulings have frequently favored Trump, liberal judges continue handing down injunctions to block his executive policies and orders.

Add to this an unprecedented degree of uncertainty about the 2026 midterm elections. More than 50 frustrated Senators and Congressmen, many of them Republicans, are choosing not to seek re-election. Most recent polling gives Democrats a 5-point advantage in the coming midterms, and the party occupying the White House historically loses 20 to 25 Congressional seats in the midterm terms.

If Democrats win back the House, another Trump impeachment is likely. The political landscape in 2027 and 2028 could become far more murky and less favorable to Trump than it is now.

But there is one way that Trump could do an end-around on all this and provide the nation a huge, maybe even transformational benefit in the process. Staying true to his roots and his campaign rhetoric dating back to 2015, Trump could announce in 2026 that he is now advocating for a convention of states under Article V of the U.S. Constitution, to adopt changes in how Washington works.

Specifically, he’d seek congressional term limits (something 87 percent of the public supports), a balanced budget amendment (something 49 of our 50 states already have), and limitations on the power of the judiciary.

All three of these are perfectly consistent with what Trump believes and has campaigned upon ever since he first tossed his hat into the presidential ring 11 years ago.

From a practical perspective, Trump’s involvement could ensure success of the movement working toward holding such a convention of states. Under Article V, the legislatures of 34 states must pass resolutions in order to hold it. There are 20 states which have already agreed to the measure. Trump carried 31 states in the 2024 presidential election. Of these, 17 have not yet approved the resolution.  

If Trump backs the measure and campaigns for an Article V convention, many of these 17 states would certainly get on board.

Trump’s involvement would also ensure success due to one other factor: growing public disenchantment with the status quo. About 80 percent of Americans feel the nation is in a political crisis. The current upheaval over internal immigration enforcement and campaigning in the midterms will only serve to exacerbate that sentiment.

The best legacy Trump can leave America on its 250th anniversary is to reform the status quo once and for all. And the only way to achieve that is through the Convention of States process.

John Kerezy is an associate professor at Cuyahoga Community College in Cleveland.

data.title: Senate to try again on shutdown deal
data.description:
Morning Report is The Hill’s a.m. newsletter. Subscribe here.

In today’s issue:

▪ Graham holds up Trump, Dems’ deal

▪ President to announce Fed chair pick

▪ ICE pulls back from Maine

▪ ACA enrollment drops

The Senate will reconvene Friday in an attempt to advance a deal to avoid a partial government shutdown after a late objection from Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) threw a curveball in the push to quickly pass the agreement.

Senators on Thursday reached a breakthrough in negotiations to stave off a shutdown with just one day to spare. Several departments are set to see a lapse in funding starting Saturday, but the deal reached by President Trump with Democrats sought to minimize the shutdown impact.

Under the terms of the agreement, the bill funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) — the most controversial measure Democrats are using to extract changes on immigration enforcement — would be split off from the other five pieces of legislation that are part of the so-called minibus.

The chamber would then also work to approve a temporary funding measure known as a continuing resolution (CR) to keep funding for DHS at current levels until Feb. 13. That’s designed to give members of both parties time to negotiate reforms to the appropriations bill for the department.

But Senate rules require unanimous consent for the package to advance quickly, and Graham raised objections on multiple issues late Thursday, forcing the upper chamber to delay the vote, The Hill’s Al Weaver reports.

“We’re not voting tonight,” Graham said after leaving Senate Majority Leader John Thune‘s (R-S.D.) office.

Graham’s main objection centers around language in the bill that would repeal a prior provision allowing senators to sue if their phone records were sought or seized during former special counsel Jack Smith‘s investigation.

That provision had been included as part of the bill to end the record-long government shutdown from the fall, allowing the handful of senators whose records were seized to sue for up to $500,000. After that bill was passed, Graham vowed he would sue, while other senators said they wouldn’t.

Members of both parties in the House took significant issue with the provision after it passed, and they included a repeal in the current must-pass funding package.

Graham, a top ally of Trump, defended his stance in favor of preserving the provision.

“I’m not going to ignore what happened. If you were abused, you think you were abused, your phone records were illegally seized — you should have your day in court,” he said. “Every senator should want to make sure this never happens again.”

The GOP senator also raised an issue with the DHS bill only funding the department for two weeks rather than the rest of the fiscal year.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) blamed Graham for the hold-up, while Thune said delays were happening from both sides of the aisle.

Thune expressed hope that Friday would have a better chance of the package advancing.

“Tomorrow’s another day and hopefully people will be in a spirit to try to get this done,” he said as he left the Capitol Thursday night. “Hopefully by sometime tomorrow we’ll be in a better spot.”

The potential for a shutdown seemed increasingly likely throughout the day Thursday, especially after the Senate initially failed to pass the six-bill package to fund the remaining parts of the government not yet covered for the rest of fiscal 2026.

But negotiations continued, leading to a deal by the evening.

Trump urged lawmakers to get behind the agreement in a post on Truth Social, saying he’s working with Congress to ensure the government can be fully funded. 

“Hopefully, both Republicans and Democrats will give a very much needed Bipartisan ‘YES’ Vote,” he said.

Even if Graham is ultimately satisfied and the deal is approved Friday, a short-term funding gap will likely still occur.

The House has been in recess throughout the week and isn’t set to return until Monday.

Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) asserted that the House plans to move forward with the agreed-upon legislation. 

“But the House is going to do its job. We want to get the government funded, as does the president, so respect whatever he was able to negotiate there, and we’ll deal with it,” Johnson told The Hill’s Judy Kurtz at the premiere of the film “Melania” at the Kennedy Center last night.

Presuming the deal passes, it would avoid what would be a shutdown affecting much of the federal government.

Congress has already approved six of the 12 annual the appropriations bills, funding covered areas of the government through Sept. 30, the end of fiscal 2026.

Still, that accounts for a minority of discretionary spending, with the bulk of the spending still at stake in the remaining six bills.

Democrats remained united in opposing the DHS bill following the killing of ICU nurse Alex Pretti in Minneapolis. Party members slammed federal immigration agents’ conduct as out of control and called for it to be reined in.

Republicans were initially divided on how to respond, but discussions started taking place between Democratic leadership and the White House midweek, leading to Thursday’s breakthrough.

But speeding this along hinges on consent from all senators, including Graham.

Smart Take with Blake Burman

Iran amped up its rhetoric Thursday as state media is reporting that it’s preparing for live fire drills in the Strait of Hormuz. That waterway is consequential for many reasons, including that roughly a quarter of the world’s oil passes through it daily. I asked Sen. Dave McCormick (R-Pa.) about what the possibility of U.S. strikes could mean for oil markets, as they are now reacting to the growing tensions.

“We may have some near-term disruption and near-term price action. I wouldn’t want to predict that,” McCormick said.

“The trend here is to create open markets and the opportunity to develop those energy capabilities in Iran, Venezuela and around the world,” he added.

Crude oil prices are up around 8 percent in the past five days. Of course, no one knows yet what Trump will decide to potentially do, but oil markets, which eventually filter down to your local pump, are already reacting.

Burman hosts “The Hill” weeknights, 6p/5c on NewsNation.

3 Things to Know Today

  1. Trump sued the Internal Revenue Service for at least $10 billion in damages over an agency contractor who leaked his tax returns to news outlets during his first term. He filed the suit along with his two oldest sons, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump, and the Trump Organization.
  2. The president threatened to impose tariffs on various countries and groups selling oil to Cuba, escalating tensions between the U.S. and the communist country.
  3. The U.S. Embassy in Copenhagen, Denmark, has reportedly reinstalled Danish flags meant to honor the country’s troops who fought in Afghanistan following widespread backlash over their removal from veterans from both countries.

Leading the Day

FED CHAIR ANNOUNCEMENT: Trump said he plans to announce his nominee for the next chair of the Federal Reserve this morning after months of speculation.

Former Fed board of governors member Kevin Warsh has reportedly emerged as the favorite for the position, The New York Times and CNN report.

Others candidates included National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett, BlackRock executive Rick Rieder and Fed Governor Christopher Waller.

Trump said during his Cabinet meeting Thursday that he planned to announce the nominee next week. But he revealed while at the premiere for the documentary film “Melania” about the first lady that he moved the timeline up to Friday morning.

At the Cabinet meeting, he reiterated his criticism of the agency for not lowering interest rates more quickly.

The term of the current chair, Jerome Powell, is set to end in May.

Trump has regularly slammed Powell, whom he appointed during his first term, for his leadership of the Fed and not being more aggressive in lowering interest rates. The Fed voted to keep interest rates at their current level at a meeting Wednesday despite the pressure from the president.

“We’re paying far too much interest in the Fed. The Fed rate is too high, unacceptably high,” Trump said Thursday.

Further adding to the intensity of the situation is the Department of Justice’s probe into Powell, which he has rejected as designed to pressure him to support lower rates.

▪ CNN: Takeaways from Powell’s press conference.

‘PERHAPS, INSURRECTIONIST’: Trump escalated his rhetoric about Pretti early Friday morning, branding the ICU nurse killed by immigration agents as both an agitator and “perhaps” an insurrectionist.

The president’s online comment references a video in which Pretti appears to engage in an altercation with federal immigration agents more than a week before he was killed.

“Agitator and, perhaps, insurrectionist, Alex Pretti’s stock has gone way down with the just released video of him screaming and spitting in the face of a very calm and under control ICE Officer, and then crazily kicking in a new and very expensive government vehicle, so hard and violent, in fact, that the taillight broke off in pieces,” the president said in a Truth Social post early Friday morning.

“It was quite a display of abuse and anger, for all to see, crazed and out of control,” Trump continued.

The comments build on Trump’s past remarks on Pretti in which he attempted to shift blame toward him for the incident that led to his death. He previously said the 37-year-old protester shouldn’t have had a gun on him at the protest.

“You can’t have guns. You can’t walk in with guns,” Trump said Tuesday.

Pretti had a license to carry the firearm and didn’t appear to take out the gun before being shot.

▪ USA Today: Millions donated to GoFundMe for Good, Pretti after shootings

▪ NBC News: Viral video plays key role in Minneapolis.

ICE ROLLBACK: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is ending its enhanced operations in Maine following a plea from Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine) to Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem, a rare step back for the administration on immigration enforcement.

“There are currently no ongoing or planned large-scale ICE operations here,” Collins said in a statement. “I have been urging Secretary Noem and others in the Administration to get ICE to reconsider its approach to immigration enforcement in the state.”

“I appreciate the Secretary’s willingness to listen to and consider my recommendations and her personal attention to the situation in Maine,” she continued.

Maine had been the latest front in the Trump administration’s ongoing immigration enforcement push, which has mostly been targeted at Democratic-leaning cities and states.

DHS launched “Operation Catch of the Day” in Maine last week. Gov. Janet Mills (D), who is running for Senate to challenge Collins, slammed the operation and requested a meeting with Trump following the killing of Pretti in Minneapolis. Mills expressed concern about a “threat to the lives of law-abiding people” in her state and across the U.S.

Immigration enforcement has been a key part of the administration’s focus through Trump’s second term, and while officials are pulling back at least for now in Maine and possibly Minnesota, they haven’t signaled a broader shift in their strategy.

Still, the decision is notable and comes as the president has faced declining approval numbers broadly and particularly on immigration, which has traditionally been a stronger issue for him with the public.

A Reuters/Ipsos poll released this week showed Trump’s approval rating on immigration falling to a record low of 39 percent.

Collins said Tuesday that she asked Noem to pause the enhanced operations in Maine so the tactics could be reviewed and made more targeted.

The GOP senator has stopped short of calling for Noem to resign, as two of her Republican colleagues, Sens. Thom Tillis (N.C.) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska), have done.

The issue of Trump’s immigration policies has also been poignant particularly in Maine as Collins is running for reelection in a Democratic-leaning state that voted for former Vice President Kamala Harris in 2024. Mills has accused the incumbent of not doing enough to help rein in ICE.

▪ The Hill: Tom Homan says Trump wants to ‘fix’ Minnesota operations.

▪ KSTP: What does a scaled-back ICE operation look like?

NOEM SILENT: The Homeland Security secretary remained quiet and the controversy in Minneapolis was never discussed during Trump’s first Cabinet meeting of the year.

Noem wasn’t the only Cabinet member who didn’t speak during the meeting, which lasted more than an hour, but it was a break from past meetings in which the president has gone around the table to have all members talk. More attention than usual has been on Noem following the shootings in Minneapolis and amid negotiations over the DHS bill.

Trump also notably didn’t take any questions during the meeting, unlike past ones in which he has frequently let reporters ask questions after everyone spoke.

Noem has faced calls from Democrats, along with a couple of Senate Republicans, to resign over the scrutiny she’s faced for her handling of the situation in Minneapolis. But Trump has said Noem will stay in the role and that he believes she’s doing well in leading the department.

▪ The Hill: Republican senator rails against Noem.

ACA ENROLLMENT DROP: The number of people who signed up for health care through the Affordable Care Act (ACA) dropped by more than 1 million as enhanced subsidies expired at the end of last year.

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services reported 23 million people signed up for open enrollment, which ended Jan. 15. That’s a drop from the 24.2 million people who signed up for insurance over the same period a year ago and ends four consecutive years of record enrollment for ACA marketplace plans.

Enhanced tax credits enacted through the American Rescue Plan, passed as a response to the COVID-19 pandemic, were credited with boosting the number of enrollments. But their expiration after lawmakers failed to reach an agreement on extending them caused premiums to skyrocket for more than 20 million Americans.

The House passed a three-year extension of the subsidies, with the support of Democrats and a few moderate Republicans, earlier this month, but that bill hasn’t advanced yet in the Senate. And the issue has largely been overshadowed recently by the national debate over the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement policies.

▪ The Wall Street Journal: Health care is more expensive than mortgages for some.

▪ CBS News: States expand state-funded subsidies to make up for lapse.

When & Where

  • The president will sign executive orders at 11 a.m. He will participate in a policy meeting at 2 p.m.
  • The Senate will meet at 11 a.m.
  • The House returns Monday.

Zoom In

DEM DONOR MONEY FLOWS IN: Democratic donors have been compelled to open their checkbooks to support their party in the wake of the recent killings of two U.S. citizens by immigration officials in Minneapolis, The Hill’s Amie Parnes reports. 

Democrats across the country have expressed outrage at the Trump administration for the response to the killing of Renee Good and Pretti in Minneapolis earlier this month.

Major Democratic fundraisers and donors told Parnes said the killings provided what one person called “a shot of cold reality” about what the country is facing under the Trump administration.

One Democratic grassroots fundraising director said that they wouldn’t be surprised to see major donors coming back online for the party soon.

This potential uptick, a rebuke of sorts to the Trump administration’s immigration practices, would be welcome news for a party that saw a lot of donors frustrated with Democrats following their crushing defeats in 2024.

Donors began to slowly come back during the fall, not because the party demonstrated it was on an improved tack but because donors were displeased by “the state of the world,” as one put it.

“I think a lot of people are realizing that if we do nothing, we cede control to Trump and enough is enough,” one donor said at the time. “It gets worse every day.”

Democrats had a decent year financially in 2025, but whether this projected increase in donor and fundraising revenue comes to fruition will remain unclear at least until contributions for the first quarter of 2026 are revealed.

Elsewhere

KIEV BOMBING PAUSED:Trump said Thursday that Russian President Vladimir Putin agreed to a temporary pause on bombing the Ukrainian capital city of Kyiv for one week.

Putin’s military has been targeting power facilities in the city and country in ways that Ukrainian officials describe as “weaponizing winter,” according to The Associated Press.

“I have to tell you, it was very nice. A lot of people said, ‘Don’t waste the call. You’re not going to get that.’ And he did it, and we’re very happy that they did it because on top of everything else, that’s not what they need is missiles coming into their towns and cities,” Trump said during a Cabinet meeting Thursday.

The president told reporters he “personally asked” Putin to stop bombing the capital on account of impending brutally cold temperatures.

Since Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine, Putin has targeted infrastructure points in the city and across the country at large. Temperatures are expected to reach as low as minus 30 degrees Celsius, or minus 22 degrees Fahrenheit, in the coming week.

The Kremlin has not yet made an official public announcement agreeing to the pause, though Trump said Thursday that Putin consented.  

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky thanked Trump for his effort and welcomed the “possibility” of a pause in Russian military action on Kyiv and beyond.

“Energy is the foundation of life,” Zelensky said in a post on the social platform X.

The remarks came as U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff was discussing the state of negotiations between the U.S. and Russia as it relates to the war in Ukraine.

Witkoff said he and the president’s son-in-law Jared Kushner made “a lot of progress” during a meeting with five Russian generals in the United Arab Emirates last Sunday.

▪ Reuters: Ukrainians face tough weeks as Russia targets power sector

Opinion

  • Congressional budget process failures defy big fix, Don Wolfensberger writes in The Hill.
  • Will Republicans break their losing streak on health care reform? former Health and Human Services Secretary Tom Price writes in The Hill.

The Closer

And finally … 👏👏👏 Congrats to this week’s Morning Report quiz winners! They’re secure in their knowledge about the Department of Homeland Security.

🧩 Here’s who went 4/4: Jess Elger, Stan Wasser, Harry Strulovici, Richard Baznik, Rick Schmidtke, Carmine Petracca, Chuck Schoenenberger, Laurie Boulden, Sari Wisch, Mark Williamson, Linda Field, Stanton Kirk, William Moore, Lori Benso, Terry Pflaumer, Dennis Barksdale, Robert Shiff, Sheila Bronfman, Michael Salanik, Robert Bradley, Sharon Banitt, Ned Sauthoff, M. Whitehouse, Steve Comer and JA Ramos.

The department was created in 2002 in the aftermath of the 9/11 attacks to better coordinate security efforts.

The National Security Agency is the only one of the choices not under DHS, as it falls under the jurisdiction of the Pentagon.

DHS is the third-largest Cabinet department by number of employees. The Department of Veterans Affairs is larger, second only to the Defense Department.

There have been eight Senate-confirmed, permanent secretaries of DHS, including Noem, though there have been several more acting secretaries. Tom Ridge was the first secretary under George W. Bush.

data.title: Trump announces Kevin Warsh as Fed chair nominee
data.description:
President Trump early Friday nominated former Federal Reserve board member Kevin Warsh to be the central bank’s next chair.

“I am pleased to announce that I am nominating Kevin Warsh to be the CHAIRMAN OF THE BOARD OF GOVERNORS OF THE FEDERAL RESERVE SYSTEM,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“I have known Kevin for a long period of time, and have no doubt that he will go down as one of the GREAT Fed Chairmen, maybe the best,” he continued later in the post. “On top of everything else, he is “central casting,” and he will never let you down. Congratulations Kevin!

Warsh, a former Morgan Stanley banker and economic adviser to former President George W. Bush, had long been considered one of the frontrunners for the president’s nomination. He served on the Fed board from 2006 through 2011, where he became the youngest governor in the bank’s history and served as its liaison to Wall Street during the 2007-08 financial crisis.

A perennial candidate for top Trump administration economic posts, Warsh was among the finalists for the president’s first Fed chair nomination in 2017. He ultimately lost out to current Chair Jerome Powell, whom he would replace if confirmed by the Senate.

Powell’s term ends in May.

Warsh, however, may have trouble getting confirmed amid Trump’s efforts to reshape the Fed.

Republican Sens. Thom Tillis (N.C.) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) have vowed to oppose anyone the president nominates to the role while the Justice Department is conducting a criminal probe into the bank and Powell.

Tillis could also use his perch on the Senate Banking Committee to hinder Trump’s Fed nominees from being approved by the panel, which is a key procedural step on the way to a full Senate confirmation vote.

Tillis and Murkowski’s support could be critical for Warsh with Senate Democrats unlikely to give him much, if any, support. Democrats are also fuming over Trump’s attempt to fire Fed board member Lisa Cook, whose challenge to the president’s order was heard last week by the Supreme Court.

A ruling against Cook could give Trump power to fire members of the Fed board and replace them with nominees sympathetic to his views, giving him unprecedented control over the central bank.

Trump has made clear that his next Fed chair nominee should be someone willing to consult him on interest rate decisions. Fed historians and analysts say such a relationship would breach the bank’s historic independence to set monetary policy based on the economic views of top officials, not the political whims of the president.

In Warsh, the president has tapped a loyal advocate with a Fed background who may willing to break the mold of his predecessors.

Warsh has accused the Fed under Powell of using independence as a shield from accountability, and said members of the bank should “grow up” and “be tough” in the face of criticism.

Warsh also shares close political connections to Trump. His father-in-law, cosmetics heir Ronald Lauder, has donated millions of dollars to Trump and Republican candidates, and was reportedly behind Trump’s quest to purchase Greenland.

Trump has frequently called his decision to nominate Powell as Fed chair one of his biggest regrets of his first term. The president has accused Powell, a Republican, of manipulating interest rates for political ends, mismanaging inflation and lacking both the intellect and fortitude to effectively manage the central bank.

Trump’s rage with Powell began soon after he took over the Fed chairmanship in 2018, and refused to heed the president’s call for crisis-level interest rate cuts. As the U.S.-China trade war heated up in 2019, Trump berated Powell for not slashing rates to give the White House more leverage in trade negotiations.

When Powell stood firm, Trump then began threatening to fire the Fed chair despite federal law protecting Fed board members from being dismissed by the president but “for cause.” 

Trump eased up on Powell when the coronavirus pandemic crashed the economy in 2020, prompting the Fed to slash rates to the president’s desired level and deploy trillions of dollars in emergency loans. But the president turned up the heat on Powell as he stormed back to the White House in 2024, and has spent most of the past year threatening to fire him again.

Powell has insisted that he will not leave before the end of his Fed chairmanship, and said the bank’s decisions under his watch have been guided exclusively by the Fed’s dual mandate of maximum employment and low, stable inflation.

data.title: Democrats have no credibility until they replace Cuellar on Appropriations
data.description:
While the Midwest and East Coast huddled against the snow, American families watched in horror as Border Patrol agents disarmed, tackled and unloaded a full clip into Alex Pretti, an American citizen and intensive care nurse with the Veterans’ Administration. Moments later, they watched as government officials lied about circumstances, despite video evidence.

Immediately, people took to social media. Many targeted their rage at the seven House Democrats who voted, alongside House Republicans, to advance the Department of Homeland Security funding bill. The bill funds immigration enforcement without real guardrails that could protect Americans.

Without these Democratic votes, the bill would not have cleared the chamber.

Generally, the minority party in the House has few tools to influence policy, but Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) regularly needs Democratic votes to advance spending bills. Given that their votes were necessary to move the Homeland Security bill, Democrats have oversized power when it comes to funding measures.

So, how did Democrats — despite their power in this specific area — allow a bill without safeguards to advance?

The responsibility for that failure belongs to Ranking Member Rep. Henry Cuellar (D-Texas). He negotiated and voted in favor of the bill, despite the killing of Renee Good and the alleged blinding of Kaden Rummler. Pretti’s recent death only compounds his guilt.

It does not appear that Democratic leadership pressured him to negotiate safeguards and his “yes” vote appears to have their tacit approval.

Since Pretti was killed , the Democratic leadership in the House has shifted its tune. Regrettably, it’s too little too late; the bill has already advanced.

Funding the Department of Homeland Security unchecked is not the only black mark against Cuellar. In 2024, he was indicted by a Houston grand jury on bribery, money laundering and conspiracy charges. The indictment alleged that he accepted $600,000 from foreign powers to influence U.S. policy.

The case will never go to trial, though, because President Trump pardoned Cuellar last month.

The timing of the pardon should trouble everyone, alongside the underlying accusation of foreign bribery.

To reassure Democratic voters of their commitment to rein in federal immigration enforcement, Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) and Appropriations Committee Ranking Member Rosa DeLauro (D-Conn.) should remove Cuellar from overseeing funding for Homeland Security and ICE, and from the Appropriations Committee.

The failure of Democratic leadership — who are already viewed as weak and ineffective — to hold Cuellar accountable — will haunt the 2026 mid-term. Did they just allow a member to trade unchecked ICE funding for a presidential pardon?

James Lewis is the former issue advocacy director for the Young Democrats of America and a former staffer to a congressional appropriator.

data.title: Democrats say they are seeing signs of a fundraising boost amid outrage over ICE
data.description:
Democratic donors say that the fatal shootings by federal agents in Minnesota this month have not only generated outrage in key fundraising circles but have compelled many to open up their checkbooks. 

In more than half a dozen interviews with The Hill, major Democratic fundraisers and donors said the shootings provided what one major check writer called “a shot of cold reality” about what the country is facing under the Trump administration.

A number of Democratic donors vowed to not give another penny to the weakened party after its brutal 2024 losses, but now some are vowing to return.

“I am predicting they won’t be able to spend all the money in the midterms,” John Morgan, a longtime Democratic mega-donor and prominent personal injury lawyer, told The Hill, projecting a surge in donations for candidates up for reelection. 

Morgan said he expects Democrats will see an influx in cash in the coming months as a result of the shootings in Minneapolis. 

In conversations with House leaders in recent weeks, Morgan said he has even come up with a pitch for other donors: “Write a check … to get a check on power.”

It will be months before contributions for the first quarter of 2026 will be revealed, but operatives at Democratic organizations say they are seeing an increase in donations from big and small donors. 

“Grassroots donations are starting to pick up, similar to how they did in Trump’s first term,” said one Democrat with knowledge of the grassroots fundraising efforts. “So it wouldn’t be surprising that major donors start to come back online.” 

It all comes at the start of an election year in which the House and Senate majorities will be at stake in November’s midterms. Democrats need only to gain a handful of seats to take back the House; winning back the Senate will be more challenging, given the states hosting this year’s races.

Congressional committees for Democrats had a decent year in 2025.

The Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee, the fundraising arm for House Democrats, began the year with more than $24 million and nearly doubled it by the end of November, ending the year with more than $46 million cash on hand. The Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee began the year with more than $11 million and ended up with almost $19 million. 

The Democratic National Committee, however, lost money. It began the year with $22 million and ended it with more than $12 million. 

Meanwhile, the National Republican Congressional Committee began the year with more than $11 million and ended with more $45 million. The National Republican Senatorial Committee started the year with nearly $3 million and finished the year with more than $16 million. 

But the Republican National Committee made the largest gains, beginning with more than $38 million and ending the year with nearly $90 million. 

In the weeks and months following the 2024 election, a number of Democratic donors and fundraisers said they could no longer contribute money to a party that not only suffered a bruising loss that cycle but wasn’t providing a narrative to voters about how they would emerge from the so-called wilderness. 

Some high-dollar donors and fundraisers said they felt betrayed by their party and the Biden campaign, which frequently sought to debunk questions about the former president’s mental acuity.

In the fall, donors began to slowly come back to the fold, not because the party had demonstrated that they had an improved tack but because donors were taken aback by “the state of the world,” as one put it. 

At the time, donors pointed to the actions taken against late-night talk show host Jimmy Kimmel, who was temporarily pulled off the air after he caused a controversy with comments in a monologue about the response to the assassination of Charlie Kirk, the conservative activist. 

They also disapproved of President Trump’s comments linking autism to Tylenol and the administration’s overall immigration crackdown. 

“I think a lot of people are realizing that if we do nothing, we cede control to Trump and enough is enough,” one donor said at the time. “It gets worse every day.” 

Still, a number of donors have doubts about the Democratic Party.

One donor expressed frustration with the “slow progress” made by the Democratic Party and said many contributors feel the same way. 

“I still see a lot of antipathy toward the Dem party because they can’t get out of their own way,” the donor explained. “They haven’t proven that they know how to win a national election. And I’m tired of being looked at as nothing but a checkbook.”

But the donor, who has received calls from anxious Democrats running for reelection, said the events of the last month may have led to a change of heart. 

“I think a lot of us take this seriously enough and realize that this is a net positive in the direction of fundraising,” the donor said. “When you see things this egregious, it’s going to change behavior, and certainly donor behavior and overall voting behavior.” 

Julia Mueller contributed to this report.

data.title: Federal school choice tax credit program struggles to get blue state traction
data.description:
So far, 23 states have joined the new federal tax-credit program that allows individuals to donate to organizations that give scholarships for private school tuition or extra support services for public school students.  

With mostly Republican states joining the program, details still need to be hammered out by the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) about how exactly the dollar-for-dollar tax credit will operate.  

The opt-in option leaves the school choice movement with work to do. Blue states look the new credit with skepticism, especially during an election year, with opponents stressing the impact it could have on public schools.

“This is really the biggest expansion of school choice in American history, particularly because it is the first federal, 50 state, national school choice opportunity that has ever existed,” said Tommy Schultz, CEO of the American Federation for Children. 

How does the program work? 

The federal tax-credit program allows individuals who donate to qualifying scholarship granting organizations to receive a dollar-for-dollar tax credit up to $1,700 starting in the 2027 federal tax year. 

Dollar-for-dollar means an individual is given a break on their taxes equal to the amount they donate, which differs from the standard tax deduction, which only reduces taxable income.  

The scholarships can be available for families who earn up to 300 percent of their area’s median income. They can cover anything that is already covered under Education Savings Accounts, which includes tuition, tutoring, special needs services, school supplies and transportation, for private or public school students. 

The states that have opted in are Alabama, Alaska, Arkansas, Georgia, Idaho, Indiana, Iowa, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, Nevada, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Virginia, West Virginia and Wyoming. 

For a scholarship-granting organization to qualify for the program, it must serve at least 10 students from more than one school, and at least 90 percent of donations must go to approved educational expenses. 

“This is such a no-brainer of a policy for a governor to look at and opt in, because all it means is that donors across their state can put money to use locally. So, if a state doesn’t opt in, every single taxpayer can still contribute and get a tax credit, it just necessarily means that their money would go to a scholarship organization that doesn’t operate within their state,” Schultz said. 

School choice groups are working on how to get the word out about the program and build up groups who want to offer these scholarships.  

The American Federation for Children and Odyssey teamed up to create the AFC Scholarship Fund, which will help national scholarship granting organizations who want to join the program. The fund has already received $10 million in donations to bring awareness of the program to families across the country.  

What are the unknowns? 

The ball is in the IRS’s court now. The agency has already sought public comment and is working on nailing down final regulations for the program set to begin at the start of next year. 

While states will get to submit a list of organizations that want to participate in the program, the IRS is not accepting that information yet.  

“The IRS has not yet drafted any of the rules, so no one could really say for sure if the scholarship granting organizations are meeting all the requirements of the statute and the regulations, because the regulations aren’t done yet,” said Leslie Hiner, vice president of legal policy at EdChoice. 

Opponents say part of the reason states shouldn’t even consider this yet is because without these rules, it is not clear that all students’ rights will be protected. 

“States who are opting in early, I think, are getting ahead of the law, on the question of, ‘Are they going to be able to ensure that this program does right by the kids in their state?’ I don’t think that’s clear yet. There are some really important questions about things like, ‘Will the IRS affirm that these scholarships are federal dollars and that nondiscrimination requirements apply to any entity that receives them?’ The schools that take these scholarship dollars should be required to comply with all kinds of nondiscrimination laws” said Zahava Stadler, project director of Education Funding Equity Initiative at New America.  

“And there are states opting in without knowing that their kids’ civil rights are guaranteed. There’s also the fact that reporting requirements aren’t clear,” she added. 

Opt-in option makes an unpredictable road ahead 

While some say this program is a slam dunk for states, which do not front the cost of it, they recognize in an election year some will hesitate to embrace the initiative.  

“There are numerous elections going on across the country, gubernatorial elections, and in some cases the state governor may decide, ‘I’m going to wait and let the newly elected Governor of the state in’ or there may be political reasons to wait until after the primaries are over. So again, the reasons why states haven’t opted in all together are many and varied,” Hiner said.  

States have also faced pressure from those who believe support of this program could inhibit public schools by diverting funds. 

“One thing we should certainly not be doing is creating a two-tier education system in America — private schools for the wealthy and well-connected, and severely underfunded public schools for low-income, disabled and working class kids,” Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) said last year before the legislation was passed. “That is not what this country is supposed to be about.”

The administration has pushed back, saying no funding will be decreased due to the tax credit.  

“This credit does not divert money from local or state taxes, which make up the large majority of school funding. Instead, the program encourages voluntary charitable giving from American taxpayers to support education services for qualifying students,” a joint fact sheet from the Education Department and the Treasury Department reads.  

Worries also mount the scholarships will only be used by upper-income individuals as $1,700 is not enough to cover the typical cost of tuition at a private school. 

This is “really not the way I think we should be spending our tax dollars in an environment where state budgets are constrained and public school are already very threatened, because, state budgets are facing down big cuts from the federal government,” Stadler said.  

Stadler said other policies such as cut to Medicare, SNAP, Trump’s immigration policies all “add up to big threats to state economies and state budgets that’s going to jeopardize public schools already, and yet, we have a federal government who literally the only new investment in K 12 education it has made is this. It has not done nothing to promise support for the public schools.”

data.title: Senate shutdown deal stalls over Graham objection
data.description:
The Senate’s race to avert a shutdown hit the skids late Thursday night as Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) placed a hold on the government funding package, forcing leaders to punt the vote. 

Senators had been hoping to vote on the so-called minibus after leaders struck a deal earlier in the day and President Trump endorsed it. Under the agreement, the Senate was set to vote on a package of five full-year funding bills and a stopgap measure funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) for two weeks.

But as lawmakers awaited word on the vote late Thursday, a fired-up Graham emerged from Senate Majority Leader John Thune’s (R-S.D.) office and declared, “We’re not voting tonight.”

Graham pointed to language in the bill that would repeal a provision allowing senators to sue if their phone records were collected as part of former special counsel Jack Smith’s probe. 

“What senator wouldn’t want notification that they’re looking at your phone?” he said.

“I fixed the problem that people had. I’m not going to ignore what happened. If you were abused, you think you were abused, your phone records were illegally seized — you should have your day in court,” Graham said. “Every senator should want to make sure this never happens again.”

The so-called Arctic Frost provision, championed by Graham, passed as part of the bill to end the historic government shutdown last year — and prompted anger in the House. The provision did not apply to House members.  The lower chamber stuck in the repeal item in the DHS funding bill, and it is slated to remain in the two-week continuing resolution to keep the department’s lights on until Feb. 13. 

The South Carolina Republican also signaled that he had an issue with the lack of full-year Homeland Security funds.

“The cops need us right now,” he said. “They’re being demonized. They’re being spat upon. They can’t sleep at night.“

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) laid blame for the delay at Graham’s feet. 

“Republicans need to get their act together,” he told reporters as he departed the Capitol for the night, declining to say whether Democrats have any holds on the bill as well. 

Thune said, however, that there were hang-ups on both sides of the aisle.

Senate leaders were attempting to fast-track the funding vote, requiring buy-in from all 100 members. That means a single senator could hold up the process.

“Tomorrow’s another day and hopefully people will be in a spirit to try to get this done,” Thune said as he left the Capitol. “Hopefully by sometime tomorrow we’ll be in a better spot.“

The holdup came hours after the White House and Senate Democrats announced a deal to peel the DHS funding bill off from the minibus. Democrats had threatened to tank what was originally a six-bill package unless DHS funding wasn’t removed in the aftermath of Alex Pretti’s killing by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis on Saturday. 

The remaining five funding bills — which cover the departments of Defense, Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, Health and Human Services, Labor and Education — run through the end of September.

“I am working hard with Congress to ensure that we are able to fully fund the Government, without delay,” President Trump wrote on TruthSocial early Thursday evening. “Republicans and Democrats in Congress have come together to get the vast majority of the Government funded until September, while at the same time providing an extension to the Department of Homeland Security.” 

Trump went on to urge a “very much needed Bipartisan ‘YES’ Vote.”

The deal came together after roughly 24 hours of talks between top Senate Democrats and White House.

Lawmakers had indicated the final hang-up was the length of the DHS continuing resolution. Democrats had sought the two-week extension — which ended up winning out — while Republicans wanted one in the neighborhood of a month or six weeks. 

“The two-week CR probably means there’s going to be another two-week CR and maybe another two-week CR after that,” Thune said earlier on Thursday. 

Senate Democrats on Wednesday rolled out three demands as part of a renegotiation of the DHS package. Headlining them were: ending roving patrols by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers and tightening the rules governing the use of warrants; boosting accountability by establishing a universal code of conduct for officers; and requiring agents to wear body cameras while prohibiting them from wearing masks. 

The Senate is expected to reconvene Friday to try again to pass the funding measure. It then needs to go back to the House, which means there will be at least a short funding lapse.

The House is slated to return Monday after a week-long recess. 

One GOP source told The Hill that House leaders are considering bringing members back on Sunday in order to pass the funding measures, but cautioned that it was one of many options under consideration and that no final decision had been made. But Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said he doesn’t expect floor action on the funding bills until Monday.

“We got a 72-hour return notice that’s in our rules, and so in our rules, and so we’ll be issuing that amount of the conference call with House Republicans tomorrow. I think the first earliest floor action we can have is Monday,” Johnson told reporters at the Kennedy Center at the premier of “Melania.” 

“So there we may inevitably be in a short shutdown situation, but the House is going to do its job,” he added. “We want to get the government funded, as does the president.”

With the five-bill minibus nearing completion, attention will now turn fully to the Homeland Security bill. 

Republican appropriators have noted in recent days that roughly 80 percent of the bill deals with non-ICE parts of government, including the Transportation Security Administration (TSA), FEMA, the Secret Service and the Coast Guard, which Trump namechecked in his TruthSocial post supporting the deal. 

They also believe it is possible to get a resolution by Valentine’s Day weekend, but indicated there would be a time crunch to do so. 

“I think we can do it. I would have preferred more time, but that was something that was not worked out by me,” Senate Appropriations Committee Chair Susan Collins (R-Maine) said. “That was worked out by the president and the leadership.”

Emily Brooks and Judy Kurtz contributed.

data.title: Texas freezes H-1B visas for state agencies, higher education amid investigation into federal program
data.description:
AUSTIN (Nexstar) — Gov. Greg Abbott announced an investigation into the federal H-1B visa program in “light of recent reports of abuse,” according to a letter signed by the governor. He directed all state agencies controlled by a gubernatorially appointed head and public institutions of higher education to freeze all H-1B visa applications until May 2027.

Those institutions are also required to submit a report to the Texas Workforce Commission by March 27 that identifies how many H-1B visa holders the agency currently sponsors, how many applications and renewals it has filed, the country of origin for the H-1B visa holders, their roles, when their visa expires, and any proof that the agency tried to fill the job currently held by a visa holder with a Texas candidate first.

The Texas Workforce Commission told Nexstar it does not currently keep track of the number of Texas employees sponsored under a H-1B visa because it is a federal program.

The freeze on new and renewal applications will remain in effect until after the completion of the 90th state legislative session, which will gavel in on January 2027.

“State government must lead by example and ensure that employment opportunities — particularly those funded with taxpayer dollars — are filled by Texans first,” Abbott said in a news release announcing the investigation.

Abbott hinted at the freeze during an appearance on the Mark Davis Show on Monday where he called the H-1B program an extraordinary controversy. “We want to make sure that our communities are not being — having people come in and take jobs that Texans can easily fill,” Abbott said on the radio show.

Nexstar asked the Governor’s Office if there was any specific example of fraud or abuse of the H-1B program. A spokesperson with his office did not point to a specific example but said there was a lot reporting around the federal program.

Republicans in Washington D.C. have filed bills to abolish the program over concerns it is taking away jobs from American workers.

The H-1B program allows companies to hire foreign nationals for “specialty occupations.” The intent was to help companies hire for specialty skills if they could not find those skills in the American workforce. The U.S. Department of Labor requires companies to pay H-1B visa holders at least the same amount paid to other workers with similar experience, or the prevailing wage, whichever is highest.

Jason Finkelman, an immigration attorney, compared attacking the program to shooting yourself in the foot. He said public universities are in desperate need for highly-skilled and qualified researchers and professors.

“Because it’s important for them to be able to teach and train US workers with the best talent they need,” Finkelman said.

NOW PLAYING

The federal government caps the number of H-1B visas to 85,000 per year, but it allows certain cap-exempt institutions to apply all year long and not be subject to that limit. Universities are an example of a cap-exempt institution.

According to federal data, Texas A&M University was awarded 214 H-1B visas in fiscal year 2025. A spokesperson for the university system said it stopped applying for new H-1B visas after the Trump required employers to pay $100,000 for each visa holder.

“We have received Governor Abbott’s directive ordering a freeze on new H-1B visa petitions at Texas public institutions of higher education, and we are fully complying with that directive,” a Texas A&M University System spokesman said in an email. “The System remains committed to protecting Texas taxpayers, complying with the law, and ensuring that Texas students and communities are served by a strong and qualified workforce.”

The Texas American Association of University Professors, which is affiliated with the Texas American Federation of Teachers, denounced the governor’s decision on Wednesday morning.

“Governor Abbott’s order will hurt the student experience and diminish the value of teaching and research at Texas institutions of higher education,” Dr. Brian Evans, president of Texas AAUP-AFT, said in a news release. “Nowhere will that impact be more pronounced than our world class university medical centers.”

“Patients don’t care where their doctors and nurses are from, as long as they’re getting the best possible care,” Evans said. “Texas needs its public universities to continue being the economic engines driving the state, not mere vehicles for Governor Abbott’s political agenda.”

Finkelman also questions what authority the governor has when it comes to a federal program.

“I’m not sure what, if anything, the governor can actually do as it pertains to limiting universities’ ability to utilize the H-1B visa program,” Finkelman said.

On Wednesday, Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton announced his office is investigating three North Texas businesses accused of abusing the H-1B visa program. Paxton’s office alleged these companies “engaged in fraudulent activity.”

“Any criminal who attempts to scam the H-1B visa program and use ‘ghost offices’ or other fraudulent ploys should be prepared to face the full force of the law,” Paxton said. “Abuse and fraud within these programs strip jobs and opportunities away from Texans. I will use every tool available to uproot and hold accountable any individual or company engaged in these fraudulent schemes. My office will continue to thoroughly review the H-1B visa program and always work to put the interests of Americans first.”
